
Graphics Engine Bible 2026
Volume II — Rendering Systems & Physically Based Rendering

Chapter 1: Modern Rendering Architecture

1.1 Introduction

Every modern graphics engine exists to solve a deceptively simple problem:
Convert a virtual world into pixels on a display.

A modern renderer is composed of multiple subsystems:
- Scene management
- Visibility determination
- Material evaluation
- Lighting
- Shadow generation
- Post-processing
- Temporal reconstruction
- GPU scheduling

1.2 Frame Lifecycle

Typical frame sequence:

Input
Simulation
Visibility Determination
Render Graph Construction
Shadow Passes
Geometry Passes
Lighting Passes
Post Processing
Presentation

1.3 CPU and GPU Responsibilities

CPU:
- Game logic
- Resource streaming
- Scene graph updates
- Command generation

GPU:
- Rasterization
- Shading
- Compute workloads
- Ray tracing

1.4 Render Graph Systems

Modern engines increasingly use render graphs.

Benefits:
- Automatic resource lifetime tracking
- Barrier generation
- Pass scheduling
- Memory aliasing

Example Pass Chain:

Depth Prepass
↓
GBuffer
↓
SSAO
↓
Lighting
↓
SSR
↓
Bloom
↓
Tone Mapping
↓
TAA
↓
Present

1.5 Deferred Rendering Fundamentals

Deferred rendering separates geometry processing from lighting.

Typical G-buffer:

RT0: Albedo
RT1: Normal
RT2: Material Properties
Depth Buffer
Velocity Buffer

Advantages:
- Efficient handling of many lights
- Material flexibility

Disadvantages:
- Memory bandwidth costs
- Transparency challenges

1.6 Forward+ Rendering

Forward+ combines forward rendering quality with tiled light culling.

Workflow:
1. Divide screen into tiles
2. Build light lists
3. Shade only relevant lights

Advantages:
- Better transparency support
- Lower memory requirements

1.7 Clustered Rendering

Clustered rendering extends tiled rendering into 3D space.

Benefits:
- Better scalability
- Improved volumetric integration
- Reduced light list sizes

1.8 Engine Architecture Principles

Core goals:

Scalability
Maintainability
Performance
Debuggability
Portability

Large engines should be designed around modular systems rather than monolithic pipelines.

End of Chapter 1.
